text: In general, the patient only requires two peripheral IVs placed and arterial line is not needed.
And if possible, it's preferable to have the patient intubated with a right main stem intubation.
If there's still some aeration of the left lung and the view is inadequate, insufflation between pressures of six to eight with a flow of one to three is generally well tolerated.
To begin the procedure, I typically like to first identify the left pulmonary ligament.
This landmark is relatively constant and is easy to find to get perspective of the left lower lobe.
It's also a good place to start with the 3mm sealing device in order to test it to ensure that it's functioning appropriately.
This section of the pulmonary ligament can be quite rapid with the
3mm sealing device as the use of multiple instruments is not necessary.
Just by grasping the ligament and sealing it a few times and then spreading through the seal, the tissues will usually tease away quite easily in a bloodless fashion.
When doing the dissection, it's important to grasp the lung and retract its cephalad to provide some tension.
then with sealing of the tissues and then gently spreading through it, the tissues can be dissected away in an efficient manner.
Although this patient had a CT scan that did not demonstrate a systemic anomalous pulmonary vessel, it's important to look for a vessel during the dissection of the pulmonary ligament
as they can be really present.
Here the left pulmonary vein is starting to come into view.
There is also an adjacent lymph node suggesting some mild lymphadenopathy.
The tissues in this area are also somewhat a bit thicker.
But with the use of the sealing device, the thickened tissue can be sealed multiple times and with gentle teasing and spreading, it will typically peel away.
In situations where the tissues do not tease away easily, the areas that have been sealed can be divided sharply with laparoscopic scissors in a bloodless fashion.
The left inferior pulmonary vein is now well visualized and at this point in time it's not necessary to get complete isolation of the vessel.
Attention may now be turned to the oblique fissure and the inferior pulmonary vein can be later be used as a landmark when the oblique fissure is not well defined.

the left upper lobe is being retracted cephalad and a totally incomplete fissure is noted.

In cases like this where the oblique fissure is not well defined,
I find that sometimes looking at the inferior pulmonary vein as a landmark can aid in determining the line of dissection to develop the fissure and dissection plane.
The approach to this dissection is well described by Dr. Rothenberg, and he has several videos that demonstrate this technique quite well.
It's essentially an anterior to posterior approach and a superficial to deep dissection.
I believe he equates it to like opening a book and turning one page to the next in order to define the fissure and expose the vasculature underneath.
With this dissection, the lung parenchyma is gently teased and spread with the sealing device.
And it's important to be dynamic with the left hand to grasp the left lower lobe and retracted cephalad.
And as the sealing device seals the parenchyma to move the left hand back adjacent to the sealer to create tension.
With these maneuvers,
it's easy to define the layer of the section, seal the tissues, and tease them apart in an efficient manner in a relatively bloodless fashion.
When I first started using the sealer, I was uncomfortable with how well it would seal the tissue and maintain hemostasis.
But after several cases, I found it to be a very efficient way to do a dissection without having to exchange instrumentation multiple times.
or manipulate the lung multiple times in order to get the right views or angles to safely go through the tissues.
Prior to using the 3mm sealing device comfortably, I relied heavily on hook cautery.
but as I've used three millimeter sealing device more I found that it is a very efficient way to dissect without exchanging instruments.
It seals very well to maintain a nice level of dissection and it can be a safer dissection as the thermal spread is very minimal.
The three millimeter sealing device has a few limitations
One is that it does not have a complete 360 rotation, so that takes a little bit of getting used to.
The other is that it does not cut, so in situations where the tissue is quite thick, sometimes it's necessary to go out with the sealing device and exchange it for scissors to divide the tissues.

Otherwise the device has made me more comfortable in cases such as this because there's very minimal thermal spread, but the tissues that it seals or the vessels that it seals, it maintains a excellent level of hemostasis.
As you can see, the fissure is starting to get defined anteriorly here and very minimal bleeding has occurred.
when the majority of the dissection was sealing and bluntly spreading through the tissues.
Here the fissure is not well defined posteriorly and so the dissection will be carried through the lung parenchyma in this area.
As the dissection is carried through the fissure in this location, it is important to be cognizant that the apical segment of the pulmonary artery is within this region.
If the line of dissection is unclear posteriorly, the lung may be retracted forward and the line of dissection may be identified along the posterior lung parenchyma.
Here the tissues are thicker and to ensure a clean dissection, the lung is sealed on either side and subsequently divided sharply with scissors.

Despite having right main stem intubation and an insufflation with pressures up to about eight, the patient did not experience any desaturation and experience very minimal level of hypercarbia.
here after the division of the lung parenchyma through the seal some mild oozing occurred and immediate hemostasis could be achieved by just resealing the parenchyma directly.

As this is the order that each of these structures are identified, they are subsequently controlled and divided in similar sequence.

compress the lung back down.

Now the fissure is nearly completed anteriorly.
The dissection is getting much closer to the pulmonary vessels.
At this point it's very important to stay superficial within the tissues and carry the dissection layer by layer.
The parenchyma again can be sealed on either side and divided with just gentle spreading or sharply with the scissors.
Now with just a little bit of suction dissection, one can almost imagine the location of where the pulmonary artery is at.
And we are zooming in to where we believe the vessels are going to be located.
So we will continue to take the dissection further layer by layer from anterior to posterior and continue to define the fissure until the vessels are seen.
The total length of the case was approximately two and a half hours.
Certainly the majority of the case was spent doing the dissection within the fissure in order to safely identify the vessels in a meticulous fashion.
So for the sake of time and the human attention span,
the dissection from anterior to posterior through the fissure will be sped up to four times its normal rate.
Finally, after great effort, the pulmonary artery is now coming into view.
As this section is carried out around this vessel, it is important to determine whether or not it is the main pulmonary artery trunk versus a distal branch.

To dissect around the vessel, the parenchyma or adventitia just anterior to the vessel can be grasped with the left hand, and with the use of the sealing device, the superficial layer can be sealed and gently divided with multiple spreads.
Again, let us take the time to appreciate the skillful camera work by Dr. Boangelo, who quite effectively withdraws the scope into the trocar when the evaporative smoke comes from the sealing action.
During the case it was unclear why so much evaporative smoke was occurring with each seal, but in retrospect we believe it was a consequence of placing electrolube on the hinges of the device.
Normally the electrolube is used to eliminate tackiness within the jaws of the device to prevent parenchyma tissues from sticking to it.
In our case, we would apply generous amounts, and I think that resulted in the evaporative smoke that would condense onto the lens of the camera.
Nonetheless, Dr. Buongiallo maintained an excellent perspective throughout the case.
Here the dissection is being carried out posteriorly and as this tissue is being taken down, care is being taken to identify the apical segment of the pulmonary artery.
The apical branch of the pulmonary artery is now coming into view.
This section will be carried out around it to isolate it completely.
The vessel is less than five millimeters in diameter and can be sealed and divided with just the use of the sealing device.
Once adequate clearance of the vessel is obtained, a proximal and distal seal will be made and then the vessel will be divided.
When dividing the vessel with scissors, it is important to first do a hesitation cut or at least a partial transection of the vessel to ensure that the seals are hemostatic.
Here I demonstrate complete transection of the vessel with the first cut.
a more inferior technique.
Another apical branch has been identified and further dissection will be carried out to isolate it.
As additional dissection is being carried out along the length of the left pulmonary artery, it is important to identify the other branches going to the left upper lobe.
The branch of the lingula is noted at the 7 o'clock position.
Once again, the apical branch is isolated, sealed proximally, and then sealed distally prior to dividing it.
Again to ensure hemostasis and that the seals are intact, first a hesitation cut to partially transect the vessel is made.
And once hemostasis is confirmed, the entire vessel is transected.

are not necessary for vessels this size and by using the sealer, once again, minimal use of additional instrumentation and minimal need for changing instrumentation is required.

the smaller branching vessels were declaring themselves quite well and so decision was made to just isolate them and seal them with the three millimeter sealing device.

With the branches of the pulmonary artery divided, attention is now turned to identifying the bronchus.
As the dissection progresses, by continually addressing the posterior attachments, additional length may be achieved along the hilum to further expose the underlying structures.
Here the bronchus is starting to come into view and it may be identified with tactile sensation as it is firmer to the surrounding tissues.
While dissecting out the bronchus, it is important to be always cognizant that just posterior to this will be the pulmonary vein.
Thus, when doing the dissection, it is important to stay up against the bronchus flush with the tissues.

Initially I wanted to isolate the trunk of the bronchus to staple it.
However, once again as the dissection was progressed, it was much easier to just isolate the distal segments and divide the bronchus there.
Since the bronchus has cartilage and is firmer than vessels, I will typically crush the bronchus to soften it, although this could be done with just a stapler.
By crushing it now, I also find it a bit easier to slide the bronchus into the jaws of the 5mm stapler device.

When using the stapler, it is important to always check the tips of the stapler to make sure that the ends are opposed to ensure that there is a good seal of the device so that there is good apposition once the staples are applied.
As the last branch to the left floral lobe is isolated, additional posterior attachments are taken down.

There are several different ways to take the inferior pulmonary vein.
Clips may be used, sealing devices, or even stapling depending upon the size of the patient.
For a patient that is four months of age and approximately six kilos, the sealing device is adequate to either take the branches distally or take the trunk of the pulmonary vein directly.
